questions.question: List comprehension warm-up: predict the output
questions.code:
nums = [1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6]
squares = [n * n for n in nums if n % 2 == 0]
print(squares)

answer.text:
Read it in loop order: for each n in nums, keep it if the condition passes, then apply the expression on the left. The solution block shows the exact plain loop it compresses.

The comprehension is not just shorter, it says intent in one line: "a new list, built from that one, filtered and transformed". Python programmers reach for it by default when the body would just be an append.

questions.question: if on the left vs if on the right: predict both
questions.code:
nums = [-2, 5, -1, 3]
a = [n for n in nums if n > 0]
b = [n if n > 0 else 0 for n in nums]
print(a)
print(b)

answer.text:
Two different ifs. The one **after the for is a filter**: it drops elements, so a has only the positives.

The one **before the for is a conditional expression** (chapter 3's ternary): every element survives, but negatives get replaced by 0, so b keeps all four slots.

Filter changes the length, transform changes the values. Needing both at once is legal too: [n if n > 0 else 0 for n in nums if n != -1].

questions.question: Flattening a grid: which order do the fors go?
questions.code:
grid = [[1, 2], [3, 4], [5, 6]]
flat = [x for row in grid for x in row]
print(flat)

answer.text:
Multiple fors read **left to right, exactly like nested loops**: for row in grid is the outer loop, for x in row the inner.

Everyone writes it backwards once, [x for x in row for row in grid], and gets NameError: row is used before its loop introduces it. When in doubt, write the nested for loop first, then transcribe it left to right.

questions.question: Dict comprehension: invert a mapping

answer.text:
Same shape as a list comprehension, but with **key: value** on the left and curly braces around it. Swapping v and k inverts the dict.

One caution: inversion assumes the values were unique. If two keys share a value, the later one silently wins the inverted slot, same right-side-wins rule as chapter 4's dict merge.

questions.question: Set comprehension, and a trap: predict both types

answer.text:
Curly braces with a bare expression build a **set**, so duplicates collapse: two a-words become one 'a'. Order in the printout may vary, sets do not promise any.

The trap is the second half: **{} is an empty dict, not an empty set**. Dicts had curly braces first, historically, and they kept them. The only way to write an empty set is set().

questions.question: The tuple comprehension that isn't: predict the type
questions.code:
nums = [1, 2, 3]
result = (n * n for n in nums)
print(type(result))
print(tuple(n * n for n in nums))

answer.text:
There is no tuple comprehension. Parentheses around a comprehension make a **generator expression**: a recipe for values, not the values themselves.

Want an actual tuple? Feed the generator to the tuple() constructor, like line four.

This asymmetry trips people because list, set, and dict comprehensions all produce their container directly. The parenthesized one is the odd sibling, and it is that way on purpose: lazy sequences turned out to deserve the shortest syntax.

questions.question: When does a generator actually run?
questions.code:
def numbers():
    print("starting")
    yield 1
    print("between")
    yield 2

gen = numbers()
print("created")
print(next(gen))
print(next(gen))

answer.text:
Calling a generator function runs **none of its body**. It hands back a generator object immediately, which is why "created" prints before "starting".

Each next() runs the body **up to the next yield and pauses there**, local state frozen in place. So the first next() prints "starting" and delivers 1; the second resumes after the first yield, prints "between", delivers 2.

This pause-and-resume is the whole idea: a generator is a function that can be suspended mid-thought.

questions.question: Use it twice: predict both prints
questions.code:
nums = [1, 2, 3]
gen = (n * 10 for n in nums)
print(sum(gen))
print(list(gen))

answer.text:
Generators are **one-shot**. sum() consumed every value, and the generator is now exhausted. Asking again does not restart it; list(gen) politely collects nothing.

No error, no warning, just a silently empty result, which makes this one of the sneakiest bugs in the family. If you need two passes, either materialize once (values = list(gen)) or rebuild the generator for each pass.

Compare: a list can be summed and listed all day. Laziness is a trade, you give up re-iteration.

questions.question: Driving a generator by hand: what happens at the end?
questions.code:
gen = (c for c in "ab")
print(next(gen))
print(next(gen))
print(next(gen))

answer.text:
next() pulls one value. When there is nothing left, the generator raises **StopIteration**, which is not an error condition, it is the protocol's way of saying "done".

for loops catch StopIteration for you, which is why you never see it in normal iteration. You meet it when driving generators manually, and the polite form is next(gen, None): give a default and it returns that instead of raising.

questions.question: A generator in a for loop: predict the output

answer.text:
The for loop calls next() behind the scenes until StopIteration, so the whole sequence plays out: three numbers, then the final yield.

Notice a generator can yield in a loop and after it, mix types, whatever. When the function body ends, iteration ends. The countdown pattern, yield inside while, is how you produce sequences whose length depends on logic rather than on an existing collection.

questions.question: The infinite generator: why doesn't this hang?
questions.code:
def naturals():
    n = 1
    while True:
        yield n
        n += 1

gen = naturals()
print(next(gen), next(gen), next(gen))

answer.text:
while True in a generator is not an infinite loop in the dangerous sense, because the body only runs **on demand**. Three next() calls means three trips around the loop, then everything pauses.

This is the practical payoff of laziness: infinite sequences, gigantic files, endless event streams all become safe to model, and the consumer decides how much to take. The one rule: never hand an infinite generator to something that tries to finish it, like list() or sum().

questions.question: Does the comprehension variable leak?
questions.code:
n = 100
squares = [n * n for n in range(3)]
print(squares)
print(n)

answer.text:
Comprehensions run in **their own scope**, so the n inside never touches your n outside. Print n and it is still 100.

Sharp contrast with chapter 3's drill: a plain for loop **does** leak its variable into the enclosing scope. Same-looking iteration, different scoping rules. Python 2 comprehensions leaked too; the fix in Python 3 is one of the quiet cleanups that made comprehensions safe to sprinkle everywhere.

questions.question: any, all, and the empty case: predict all four
questions.code:
nums = [1, 2, 0, 3]
print(any(n > 2 for n in nums))
print(all(n > 0 for n in nums))
print(all([]))
print(any([]))

answer.text:
any() asks "at least one truthy?", all() asks "every one truthy?", and both **short-circuit**: any stops at the first True, all stops at the first False. Feeding them generator expressions means unvisited elements are never even computed.

The empty cases are the interview favorite: **all([]) is True** and any([]) is False. "All zero of them pass" is vacuously true, the same convention as math. It matters in real code: all(user.is_admin for user in users) is True when users is empty, which may quietly grant an empty room admin rights.

questions.question: Why no brackets? Predict, then explain the style
questions.code:
words = ["hi", "there", "world"]
print(sum(len(w) for w in words))
print(max(len(w) for w in words))

answer.text:
When a generator expression is the **only argument** to a function, its parentheses double as the call's parentheses. sum(len(w) for w in words) is complete as written.

You could write sum([len(w) for w in words]), but the list version builds all the lengths in memory first, then adds them. The generator version feeds sum one value at a time, nothing stored. Same answer, smaller footprint, and it reads cleaner. This shape, aggregate(genexp), is everyday idiomatic Python.
